name: HEXACO Model (Ashton & Lee)
core_claim: HEXACO is a six-factor model of personality structure derived from cross-language lexical studies (analysis of how personality-descriptive adjectives cluster across many languages). It retains five dimensions close to the Big Five (Emotionality, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Openness to Experience) but adds a sixth — Honesty-Humility (H), with facets Sincerity, Fairness, Greed-Avoidance, and Modesty. The central claim is that a sixth, ethics-flavoured factor recurs across languages and is not adequately captured by the Big Five, and that low Honesty-Humility is essentially the inverse pole of the 'Dark Triad' (Machiavellianism, narcissism, psychopathy). For media preference the theoretical link is twofold: (1) Openness drives the same aesthetic/complexity preferences it does in the Big Five (appetite for ambiguity, art-house, unconventional narrative); (2) the distinctive H factor is hypothesised to predict moral-narrative orientation — appetite for, or discomfort with, stories that valorise self-interest, corruption, manipulation, and amoral antiheroes. A viewer low in H is theorised to find power-corrupts, con-artist, and morally-grey-protagonist content more congenial (parasocial alignment with the schemer); a viewer high in H is theorised to prefer narratives where fairness, comeuppance, and moral order are restored. This moral-orientation signal is the unique thing HEXACO offers over Big Five for content modelling — but see predictive_validity_for_media: it is currently a theoretical inference, not an established empirical media-preference finding.
predictive_validity_for_media: Weak-to-absent DIRECT evidence; the predictive case is largely inferential. (1) The five HEXACO dimensions overlapping the Big Five inherit Big-Five media findings — most notably Openness, which in genre-preference studies correlates with preference for complex, unconventional, aesthetically demanding content (typical r ~ 0.2-0.3). A representative 2025 cross-sectional film-genre study (N=300) used the Big Five (BFI-44), not HEXACO, and found e.g. Openness positively correlated with Mystery (r=0.30) and Romance (r=0.34); this is the kind of effect HEXACO's Openness would replicate. [uncertain] (2) The unique Honesty-Humility factor has NO well-established direct correlation with film/TV genre or thematic preference in the peer-reviewed literature found — media-preference research overwhelmingly uses the Big Five, and HEXACO-specific media studies are scarce. The link to moral/corruption-themed content is extrapolated from H's robust correlations with real-world behaviour: low H ~ Dark Triad traits, materialism, willingness to exploit/cheat, and lower fairness motivation (Fairness facet is the strongest restraint on unethical behaviour). [uncertain] (3) No located evidence on completion rate, narrative-style preference, or viewing enjoyment specifically tied to H. Net: HEXACO predicts media preference about as well as the Big Five via its shared dimensions, plus a PLAUSIBLE BUT UNVALIDATED H-driven moral-narrative signal. Treating the H-to-corruption-content link as established would overstate the evidence.
instrument_design: Standard self-report Likert (1 = strongly disagree to 5 = strongly agree), agree/disagree statement format. Main forms: HEXACO-PI-R, 200 items (also 100-item variant) — the full research instrument, ~24 facets, far too long for onboarding. HEXACO-100 (Lee & Ashton, 2018) — psychometrically strong, still long. HEXACO-60 (Ashton & Lee, 2009) — 10 items per factor, the most-used short form, good internal consistency and self-other agreement (convergent self-observer correlations averaging above .50); ~7-10 minutes. There are even shorter research forms (e.g. a 24-item version, 4 items/factor) but reliability degrades, especially for the multifaceted H factor. For a sub-2-minute, 6-10 item onboarding, the realistic minimum is roughly 1 item per factor (6 items) or 2 items on the dimensions that matter most for media (Openness, Honesty-Humility) plus 1 each on the others (~8 items). At 1 item/factor the score is a coarse single-indicator proxy with no facet resolution and low reliability — usable as a soft prior, not a measurement. [uncertain] (minimum-viable item counts for a 6-10 item media-tuned subset are an engineering judgement, not a validated short form.)
cold_start_applicability: Moderate and identical in mechanism to the Big Five: it is a self-report trait instrument requiring zero viewing history, so it yields signal before the first interaction — its core appeal for cold-start. The shared dimensions (especially Openness) give a defensible first-pass prior on tolerance for complexity, ambiguity, and unconventional structure, supporting cross-medium inference (a personality-derived taste for slow, ambiguous narrative should transfer from a film prior to a TV/anime catalogue). The DISTINCTIVE cold-start value HEXACO claims over Big Five is the Honesty-Humility prior on moral-narrative orientation — but because that link is unvalidated for media (see predictive_validity_for_media), its cold-start signal quality is speculative. In practice the extra sixth item buys at most a weak, unproven moral-tone prior. Signal quality before first interaction: comparable to Big Five for the overlapping dimensions; uncertain/unproven for the H increment. [uncertain]
group_compatibility_use: This is HEXACO's most interesting angle for HelpME2C's two-person core use case, though still evidence-thin. Profiles are continuous six-dimensional vectors, so the same aggregation strategies as any trait model apply: averaging (blend both profiles, recommend to the midpoint), intersection/AND (recommend only where both profiles agree), and least-misery (avoid content that strongly mismatches the more sensitive partner — useful for aversive content like existential-horror or heavy moral-compromise where one partner's low tolerance should veto). The HEXACO-specific finding worth leaning on: in partner-preference research, Honesty-Humility shows the STRONGEST assortative/similarity preference of all six factors (singles most want a partner who matches them on H and Openness; similarity moderate for A and C; weak for Emotionality and Extraversion). Higher H is also associated with greater relationship satisfaction and commitment. Implication for joint recommendation: two people are likely to be CLOSE on H and Openness, so for the dimensions that most plausibly drive moral-narrative and complexity preference, averaging and intersection should converge and produce confident joint picks; divergence on H within a pair is rarer but, when present, is a strong signal to use least-misery on morally-transgressive content. No located study directly tests which combination strategy predicts JOINT VIEWING satisfaction — this is an inference from assortative-similarity data, not a validated joint-recommendation result. [uncertain]
quiz_ux_implications: Suitable for direct Likert elicitation for the five non-H dimensions, with the same caveats as Big Five: acquiescence bias (mitigate with reverse-keyed items) and some social-desirability pull. The Honesty-Humility factor is the problem child for a consumer quiz: its items are transparently about being honest, fair, non-greedy, and modest, so they are HIGHLY susceptible to social-desirability and deliberate faking — almost nobody self-rates as manipulative or greedy when they sense they are being judged, and H is the most fakeable HEXACO dimension in selection contexts. For onboarding this means a directly-asked H item ('I would never accept a bribe') yields low-variance, ceiling-piled, near-useless data. To get honest H signal it should be disguised/indirect (e.g. preference choices, situational/forced-choice trade-offs, or — better for this product — inferred passively from in-app content/theme choices rather than asked at all). Recommended format to minimise friction and maximise signal: gamified forced-choice or 'this-or-that' content/scenario items rather than agree-rating, especially for anything touching H. [uncertain] (severity of H faking in a low-stakes entertainment onboarding, versus a hiring context, is plausibly lower but not measured.)
cross_cultural_validity: This is HEXACO's strongest credential and a genuine advantage for HelpME2C's multilingual catalogue. The model is itself the OUTPUT of cross-language lexical research: the six-factor structure, including Honesty-Humility, emerged consistently across reanalysed lexical studies in (at least) Dutch, French, German, Italian, Korean, Polish, Hungarian, Croatian, Filipino, Greek, and Turkish. The HEXACO-100 has been subjected to a large-scale measurement-invariance test across 16 languages; the HEXACO Adjective Scale shows strong invariance across Dutch/French/English; French and Korean versions are separately validated. Note the usual ceiling: studies typically find configural and metric invariance reliably but scalar invariance is sometimes NOT met (so cross-language mean comparisons are risky, though within-person structure and rank-ordering hold). Crucially for HelpME2C, Korean is one of the original derivation languages — relevant given a K-content-heavy catalogue. Coverage of Chinese, Hindi, Arabic, Spanish, and Portuguese in the located lexical/invariance work is thinner/not confirmed here, so non-derivation languages should be treated as probable-but-unverified. [uncertain] HEXACO is materially better positioned cross-culturally than instruments derived solely from English/Western samples.
privacy_consent_implications: Personality profiling is sensitive. Under GDPR, inferred personality data can edge toward Article 9 special-category territory because traits can imply or be combined to infer health (e.g. Emotionality/neuroticism as a mental-health proxy), beliefs, or political leaning — and HEXACO's Honesty-Humility is uniquely fraught because it is, by construction, a moral-character judgement (low H ~ dishonest/exploitative): storing a per-user 'this user scores low on honesty' value is a reputationally and legally hazardous inference about an individual's morality. Recommend: treat any collected HEXACO data as requiring explicit, granular, purpose-limited consent; prefer transient use (compute a content prior, do not persist raw H scores) or store only a coarse, non-moralised derived preference signal rather than the labelled trait; never expose H back to the user as a morality score. Re-identification and sensitive-inference risk are real if profiles are stored alongside identifiers. This intersects HelpME2C's ADR-0012 deletion/anonymisation rules and the §4 stop-and-ask on privacy-control flows. [uncertain] (exact Article 9 classification of inferred personality is legally unsettled; treat conservatively.)
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: Mapping by dimension (high-confidence for Openness via Big-Five overlap; SPECULATIVE for Honesty-Humility, the HEXACO-unique mapping). LOW Honesty-Humility (the distinctive HEXACO signal) -> predicted preference for: power-corrupts, corruption-and-cover-up, complicity-with-evil, moral-compromise, rise-and-fall, heist-and-caper, satire-of-power, vigilante-justice, morally-grey protagonists; comfort sitting with amoral schemers and antiheroes. HIGH Honesty-Humility -> predicted preference for: redemption-arc, guilt-and-atonement, betrayal-and-forgiveness, vengeance-and-its-cost (where the cost is moralised), found-family, friendship-tested; preference for restored moral order and earned comeuppance; likely lower appetite for celebratory-corruption content. HIGH Openness -> nonlinear-storytelling, earned-ambiguity (slow-burn, character-study), magical-realism, absurdist-comedy, meditation-on-time, meditation-on-death, cosmic-horror, alternate-history, first-contact. HIGH Emotionality -> grief-and-loss, unrequited-longing, parent-child-rift, loneliness-and-isolation, trauma-survival; likely aversion to graphic existential-horror/survival-and-endurance (least-misery candidate). HIGH Extraversion -> ensemble-mosaic, heist-and-caper, chase-and-escape, comedy. HIGH Agreeableness -> found-family, platonic-soulmates, friendship-tested; lower appetite for relentless toxic-relationship/betrayal content. HIGH Conscientiousness -> workplace-procedural, mystery-investigation, whodunit, underdog-rise. The Honesty-Humility-to-moral-theme mappings are the value-add and the part most needing validation. [uncertain]
implementation_recommendation: PARTIAL include, low priority, validate-before-trusting. Recommendation: do NOT adopt HEXACO as the onboarding backbone over the Big Five purely for the five shared dimensions — they buy the same signal at the same cost, and the media-preference literature is built on the Big Five (more transfer evidence, more genre-correlation data). The ONLY reason to prefer HEXACO is the Honesty-Humility factor as a moral-narrative-orientation prior, which is genuinely distinctive and maps cleanly onto HelpME2C's rich moral/power theme vocabulary (power-corrupts, corruption-and-cover-up, moral-compromise). If included: add a single, INDIRECT/forced-choice H-flavoured item (a moral-trade-off or content-preference scenario, never a direct 'I am honest' Likert, which will ceiling out) to whatever Big-Five-style core quiz is used, placed late in onboarding, and treat the output as a soft, low-weight prior on moral-tone — not a stored morality score (see privacy). Better still: derive the H/moral-tone signal PASSIVELY from early in-app theme choices rather than asking, sidestepping both faking and privacy exposure. Hard dependency: the H signal is only worth anything once the theme vocabulary's moral cluster is wired into scoring; without that, the sixth item is dead weight. Before relying on the H-to-content link in production, run an internal validation against actual preference data — it is currently theory, not measured. Cross-cultural strength is a real tie-breaker if a six-factor model is wanted for the multilingual catalogue. [uncertain]
key_sources: Ashton & Lee (2007) 'Empirical, theoretical, and practical advantages of the HEXACO model' (Personality and Social Psychology Review) — foundational, often paywalled. Ashton & Lee (2009) 'The HEXACO-60: A short measure of the major dimensions of personality' (Journal of Personality Assessment; PubMed PMID 20017063) — the key short form. Lee & Ashton (2018) 'Psychometric Properties of the HEXACO-100' (Assessment; SAGE, paywalled). hexaco.org — official site, FREE items, scoring, translations, reference list. Wikipedia: 'HEXACO model of personality structure' and 'Honesty-humility factor' — free overview. Cross-cultural: 'The HEXACO-100 across 16 languages: a large-scale test of measurement invariance' (ResearchGate, free PDF); HEXACO Adjective Scale cross-cultural validity (Journal of Personality Assessment 2025, PubMed PMID 41268970). Dark-Triad link: 'The dark core and honesty-humility...' (PMC PMC12163586, free). Partner similarity: 'Singles' similarity preferences in an ideal partner' (Frontiers in Psychology 2023, free) and Liu et al. 2018 (European Journal of Personality). Rec-systems context: 'Big-Five, MPTI, Eysenck or HEXACO: The Ideal Personality Model for Personality-aware Recommendation Systems' (arXiv:2106.03060, free — full text could not be parsed here so its specific HEXACO verdict is unconfirmed). Film-genre effect sizes cited are from a Big-Five study (PMC12182957), not HEXACO.
